speaker: interviewer
text: September 16th, 1996. So Anna Segova wanted you to stay as her child, I suppose.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though you are in your 20s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you decided not to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I don't-- for sure, I want to see, you know, who's alive from the Jewish people. And maybe I even find somebody. Maybe somebody is alive. And I didn't want to stay in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you know what happened to your mother at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said everybody's dead, sure. Everybody said that everybody's dead. The, the-- it was they, they said when they buried. Then I went to the, there the grave. And we put them ornament. And I haven't looked today for the picture. But somewhere, I have it. But I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You put up the monument in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I mean-- oh, no. Segova was-- no. For the, a monument here in Montreal already.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- in Luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right after.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right after.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- as many Jews it was came from Russia, from our town or who was hidden. So everybody got together. And we paid in some way-- how much it cost. We took some from the rail, railway station. You know, those same long iron which they, they-- you know, the railway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the tracks-- and they put into with a board. And this 20,000 Jews were from Luck and buried. So-- and it came. And we candle the light and the, the candles and everything. So-- and I have a picture of this. But I don't know where it is. Somewhere, I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came out of hiding in Luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw other Polish-- what was the very first thing you did when you came out of hiding? What was the very first thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first step, we went out to see if it's day or night. We didn't know. You know, to, to try to look on the eyes. And I didn't have what to wear. So somebody gave me a pair of wooden clogs. And Segova gave me something to wear, you know, just the-- and then that's it. I went out. And I met a couple of people from my town which they knew my parents and this and that. So-- and then we run away to, to Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Jewish people that you met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people, yeah-- so everybody is-- and I didn't go in into the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I couldn't take it. I met a man who used to bring water to us when I walked. And he came over. And he recognized me. You know, he was used to bring water and, you know, with the a [INAUDIBLE]. This is from the film, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: To be the milkmaid.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the two pails. So he said, no, nobody's alike. Nobody lives and don't go. And I didn't go in. And I went away. It's about 50 kilometers from my us. And I went to Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Rovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is-- was also a city like Luck. And everybody got together. It was closer to the Russian border. And people came from Russia. So they came also to Rovno. And everybody met-- you alive? I'm also alive. You alive? I'm also alive. You know, this is the way. Then I used to travel back and forth to Segova to the lady, used to bring her-- because I started to work. And I got dressed. And I start to be a mensch.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bookkeeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn bookkeeping?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew it. It's still from Luck. I knew it. And I knew it very well. I didn't start right away. But I started the cashier. And I worked. I was able to keep up with myself. And I and another girl, we rented a one-room apartment in, in Rovno. And it was-- it was not bad. And then I went-- my cousin came from, from-- he was in the army, in the Polish army. And he came. He was in to take me. Somebody told him that I am alive. So he was looking for me. He came to Luck. They told him I was there. But I went to Rovno. He came to Rovno. I was not at home. So he left a number of the PO box from the army. And this, we got in touch. I didn't see him. And after this, I went after maybe a year. I was in Rovno. And I went to meet him there in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to, to Warsaw. And I was there with him till he got married. A friend of my-- met a girl. And I said, you know what? I have in my cousin. He's single. He's in the army. He's this and that. Came and sleep over-- and you going to meet him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, it went to a [INAUDIBLE], you know? And they got married. And then they got married. I said, now I'm free. I can go. So and I went-- came my other cousin in from Russia. And he said, we're going to leave Poland when after they [INAUDIBLE] when it was in [INAUDIBLE]. We're going to leave Poland. So he said, if you going to go with him, so I permit you to go. But otherwise, he didn't let me go. I wanted to go before, but he didn't. So I went with him to Poland. And from there, you know, to all those till I came in Hofgeismar in Germany. And I met my husband. And that's-- and this is the story. [LAUGHS] When did you get to-- when did you leave Poland for the last time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get from Poland to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also through [INAUDIBLE] and through-- all over there, the, the camps and all over-- you know, with the illegal, With the [INAUDIBLE]. So-- and this is the way I came to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- so you left Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was in the-- you moved out illegally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember anything about that, how it was? Or did you have to go under false papers, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no papers at all, no papers at all-- the opposite. We had to throw away all the papers, ours, which I had. So and we came. And I landed in Germany. And, and the rest the story, you know from my husband. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But how-- I'd like to know, how did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I met my husband-- you think I going to tell different? We met him [LAUGHTER] in the bathroom, waiting for the line to go into the toilet. This is the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing-- before you met your husband in the camp, what did you do in the camp in Hofgeismar?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my cousin. Because he was the leader of the camp. And I cooked for him. And I cleaned and washed, you know? And I was there. And there, I met a lot of people. And we were busy. And I took some German lessons. You know, you have to know-- German lessons, and English a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what was your-- when you got married to your husband, what was your wedding like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wedding? What shall I tell you? It was not like a wedding here. He, he with his sister, they arranged. Because I didn't have nobody to arrange for me. I didn't have even-- some friends supposed to come from Germany, from Foehrenwald to be, you know, like to give out the bride-- not family, but just friend. They left a kid. So they like a little girl, a baby girl. They left her a babysitter. And they came. Because I knew them. And I asked them, because I didn't have nobody. And this-- the wedding, so it was in a house which our neighbor gave the house. They have a big house. And, and he arranged some meat, you know-- somebody should deliver. And then they didn't deliver, they didn't-- couldn't get and all kind. But anyway, it was the wedding. And it was a meal. It was OK. Somebody gave me a piece of material for a dress. It was a dentist doctor in, in the camp. So they gave me a piece of material.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color?

speaker: interviewee
text: Silk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beige-- I don't know, something. So and then a lady-- my sister-in-law, his, his sister's friend, said she's a good dressmaker. She going to make it. And she made it. It was just terrible. But anyway, it was OK. And she made a dress. And I had her dress. And it's worse, and thank God. It's 48. Another two years, I hope we're going to live, going to celebrate the 50th wedding-- if.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there is-- then afterwards, you came to Canada with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? When did you come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Novemeber the 1st, the end of October, I think, October the 30th or November the 1st, 1948, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like in Canada when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I, I like in Canada?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what was it like? What was life like in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you see, I didn't-- I was-- I just, when we went there. He went. He arranged a room. We lived on the [INAUDIBLE], rented on the [INAUDIBLE] till the man decided that he going to give us the house, because I'm not pregnant. Because he said, you have to come. I want to see your wife. And then they didn't let us. I said, we're going to move from there. Because it was on the third floor in a small gallery room with one iron bed. And then he said, you can stay as long as you want. And she took me to a butcher opposite the street in Montreal. And she taught me to buy a pound of mincemeat. At the time, it cost $0.35. And you should make for you and your husband five meals.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to make hamburgers and give them to work, a hamburger sandwich, and then came back-- also a hamburger sandwich. At the beginning, we didn't have money. And she taught me, here is the kosher butcher. You go in. She went in with me. And she taught me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then another lady gave me a coat, because I came. I didn't have what to wear and nothing. And we managed. And then I went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: As, as what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Button-- I sew buttons for $0.50 an hour in a factory. And then when I got pregnant and I gave birth to my son, the factory, all the workers, came to the bris. And they gave me beautiful presents for my son. And they wanted-- and even now, I meet those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: You.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two.

speaker: interviewee
text: A son and a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is Abraham-- Abraham Isaac, after both fathers. And my daughter is Rachel Sylvia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, after we-- both mothers. Yes, my son has four girls. And my daughter has a son-- a girl and a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to get back a little, do, do you still think of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still think of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the war? Sometimes-- I always dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thinking, thinking, thinking, how, how? What happened to me? How I made it? Was I smart? Was I stupid? And that I did it, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you, do you know why you survived? Do you have an idea why you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have no idea why I survived, why I'm the chosen one. I have-- I don't know. Who choose me? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you want to leave, that you want to tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell? Thank God I have two children. I have grandchildren. And at least somebody will remember us. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Ingber. I appreciate this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Thank you. It was not easy. I think it's the first time for all the times that I spoke about this so long.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I'm glad it's over. [LAUGHS] This is what I'm glad.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- this is the picture of my father. He was, I think so, that he was 20 years old, taken in Luck. And his name was Abraham Paszt. This is a picture of my father's brother. [? Slulig ?] Paszt is his name. And this is his wife. And the age, I don't know. But they both-- I find their picture here in the United States. So that's why I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: His [? Slulig ?] Paszt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name, your aunt's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is [? Sulka, ?] yeah, [? Sulka ?] Paszt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- and what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To them? They probably got killed. They are no more alive. They got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Luck. This is a picture from my group from Luck girls, with me in the middle. And I got this picture in Israel by one of my friends, which I find this. And he gave it to me. I was then 12 years old. This is me, Fania Paszt. It was taken in Lusk right after when I went-- I got out from the attic in 1944. And the coat-- it's not a mink coat. But it's a coat which somebody lend it to me. And the scarf, the same thing. And the scarf on the head is also. And I had there a pair of clogs, wooden clogs. And this was taken, I think, in February or in March, 1944. This is Fania and Wolfe Ingber when we got married in Hofgeismar, the 17 of April, 1948. This is Fania Ingber. This picture was made in [? Grohn ?] before we went on the boat to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you have in your hand?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I have the [INAUDIBLE]. This is the visa which we got.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Canada, yeah, it's that. This is Fania and Wolfe Ingber. This was taken in [? Grohn ?] in 1948 before we went on the boat to come to Canada. And this picture was taken in Poland, Anna Segova [INAUDIBLE]. With-- and there is one of the survivors, Mrs. Guttman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a photograph of the grave of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Anna Segova. This is my son, Abe Ingber. And he was one-year-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in, in Montreal. This is my daughter, Rachel, Rachel Ingber, when she was five years old, in Montreal. This is my son, Rabbi Abe Ingber. A He lives in. Cincinnati he has four girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken where and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cincinnati in 1996. This was taken in 1996, yes. This is [? Baba ?] and [? Zaida ?] Ingber with our first granddaughter, taken September in 1981 in Cincinnati, Ohio.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avital-- and her name is Avital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ingber. This is my daughter, Rachel, Rachel Ingber. Now, her name is Rachel Schachter. And this is her husband, Howard Schachter. And this is her two children, Davida Schachter and Joshua Shachter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when and where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in February-- the end of February, 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ottawa. This is my six grandchildren, taken at Avital Ingber's bar mi-- bat mitzvah. On top, there is Avital Ingber. Then it is Dorit Ingber.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the printed dress. And then in the dark dress is Davida Schachter from Ottawa. Then in the low, it's Tamar Ingber in the printed dress. And then is Arielle Ingber. And then is our grandson, [? Joshie ?] Schachter, in Ottawa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1992-- '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, Grandma and [INAUDIBLE]. Here is a picture of your four beautiful girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Avital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dorit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tamar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arielle.

speaker: interviewee
text: We love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a minute. Where's my husband? I talk-- this is my four girls, from my four granddaughters from Cincinnati.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- they took it in Cincinnati. And they send it to [? Baba ?] and [? Zaida, ?] we should remember. So every morning, we put on. And we listen the, the voice from the girls. Ask whatever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name is and?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Fania. I'm Fania Ingber. And right beside me is my husband, Wolfe Ingber. Wolfe, you do the talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what do you want me to say, [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We are, we are married 48 years-- [NON-ENGLISH], they say. And we hope to go on whatever is destined for us. We a remnant of great families that are no more and a remnant of a great civilization that was almost 1,000 years old in Eastern Poland. It was built and put together like a beautiful building, brick by brick. Generations build it and build it. And it had its own institutions, a culture, a beautiful language, a literature, and above all, memories and color that cannot be duplicated nowhere in the world. And the poverty of our life and surrounded by, by the hate of, of the people that we lived. Still, despite of everything, came from our midst great scholars, Talmudic scholars, and in my time, great poets, people of letters whose poetry is so beautiful that unless you can read it in the mother tongue, you can not imagine the, the lyrical beauty of it. And it is now already confined to those who really can, can fathom it. But I can testify that in the most adverse conditions from the Eastern European Jewry came out great thinkers-- from European Jewry as well. But I was born in Shebreshin. And I was raised between the two, the two world wars. My mother-- the lullaby that my mother sang to me was a war song about a soldier who fell on the battlefield. And the crow came to pick his eyes. And he says, don't pick at my eyes. This was the song, the lullaby my mother sang to me. Take my hands. And the dead soldier asked the crow, if you fly back, don't tell my mother that I am dead. For of course, she will cry all her lives. Don't say anything. Just don't take my eyes. Just pick on my hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with this song, I went to sleep every night when I was a child. And my mother's love cannot not be duplicated, or her devotion. And this was my fate, to be born and raised between two-- the First and the Second World War.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the memories of my hometown-- my mother's Sabbath candles, my father's prayers. And the, the holidays that were so beautifully celebrated in poverty but with color in our homes follows me the rest of my days. My mother's goodness, her mercy, and her love is my only consolation. And I hope that when my-- when my last day comes, I'll find solace in her love, in her memory. And I thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have anything else to add, Mrs. Ingber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to add anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, thank you very much. You had-- you did a great job. And it's-- was not easy for me. But anyway, I did it, bad or good.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you did very well, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you.
